title: Acts 12
subtitle: Herod Kills James; Peter Imprisoned and Freed

sections.header: Verses 1—17 — James Killed; Peter Arrested and Freed by an Angel

heb.paragraph: from ekteinō — to stretch out. The church was 'stretched out' in prayer toward God for Peter (v.5). The adverb describes intense, persistent, urgent prayer — not casual petition but the posture of people who believe the outcome matters and that God can change it.

the iron gate opened 'automatically' (v.10) — the only NT use of this word, giving us our English 'automatic.' Luke uses the precise Greek term for self-acting mechanisms to describe what is clearly a divine act — the language of mechanism describing the act of God.

places.name: Mary's House — Jerusalem
places.text: The house of Mary the mother of John Mark (v.12) was large enough to host a significant prayer gathering — possibly the same upper room (1:13; Luke 22:12). It was a known community gathering point; Peter goes there instinctively on his release. John Mark's family home is the Jerusalem church's recurring meeting place.

places.name: The Iron Gate
places.text: The gate leading from the prison area into the city (v.10) — likely the northern gate of the Antonia fortress adjacent to the Temple Mount. Its 'opening by itself' is the chapter's most dramatic detail: maximum security yields to divine purpose.

tl.name: Herod Agrippa's Persecution
tl.text: Herod Agrippa I (grandson of Herod the Great) arrests James and Peter to please the Jerusalem population. James is executed — the first apostolic martyrdom. Peter is miraculously freed the night before his trial.

refs.note: The first angelic prison escape — the same pattern repeated with more dramatic detail. God's method of delivering his servants is consistent: angels, opened gates, bewildered guards.

James and John had asked for seats at Jesus's right and left hand (Mark 10:37). Jesus promised they would drink his cup (Mark 10:39). James is the first apostle to fulfil that promise — martyred by Herod Agrippa.
"But the word of God continued to spread and flourish" — the chapter closes with this growth summary immediately after Herod's death: every attempt to stop the Gospel ends in its acceleration.

notes.note: MacArthur on James's martyrdom: Luke records it in two words without commentary, miracle, or apostolic rescue. MacArthur uses this to address the question of why God sometimes delivers and sometimes allows death. Both are within his sovereign will; neither represents abandonment. The same God who freed Peter from prison is the God who permitted James to die — and both outcomes served his purposes.

MacArthur on earnest prayer: the contrast between the hopeless security of Peter's prison (four squads, two chains, two guards attached to him) and the church's persistent prayer is Luke's visual argument for the power of prayer. The church had no natural hope; prayer was not a last resort but their first and only resource.

MacArthur on the prayer meeting's unbelief: this is one of the most honest portraits of faith in the NT — people praying for something they do not quite believe possible. MacArthur notes this does not invalidate the prayer: God answers earnest prayer even when the pray-ers are surprised by the answer. Faith mixed with doubt is still faith.

Calvin on James's death without angelic rescue: God permits his servants to be killed to show that the church's existence does not depend on any individual. The church survived James's death; it will survive every subsequent martyrdom. The word of God is not bound by any human life.

Calvin on the church's earnest prayer: the posture of prayer is itself an act of faith — it is the acknowledgment that God can do what we cannot. Calvin notes that 'earnestly' (ektenōs) implies both persistence and urgency: prayer that does not persist does not really believe that God is both willing and able.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that four squads of four soldiers each (sixteen soldiers total) for one prisoner represents extraordinary security — typically the maximum Roman military guard for high-value prisoners. Herod's precaution after the previous apostolic escape (Acts 5:19) is historically plausible.

"Tell James and the other brothers and sisters about this" — the NET notes this James is not the son of Zebedee (just executed, v.2) but James the brother of Jesus, who has become the leader of the Jerusalem church (Gal 1:19; 2:9). This is the first mention of James the Lord's brother's leadership role in Acts.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: "The church was earnestly praying to God for him" — Robertson notes the imperfect tense (ēn proseuchomenē — was continually praying): not a single prayer meeting but sustained, ongoing intercession throughout Peter's imprisonment. The church's prayer is persistent, not perfunctory.

"It must be his angel" — Robertson notes this reflects the Jewish belief that each person had a guardian angel who could appear in their likeness (cf. Matt 18:10). The community assumes Peter's angel has come to announce his death, not that Peter himself stands at the door. The response is poignant: they believe the answer to prayer is less likely than an angelic apparition.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Keener provides historical context for Herod Agrippa I (37–44 AD), grandson of Herod the Great. Agrippa had gained control of the whole of Judea by AD 41 — the largest Jewish kingdom since the Hasmoneans. His persecution of the church was politically motivated: it pleased the Jewish population (v.3) and thus consolidated his domestic support.

Keener notes the significance of Mary's house. John Mark is Barnabas's cousin (Col 4:10), connecting this Jerusalem household to the Antioch mission. The house was substantial — an outer gate (pylōn) and a servant (paidiskē) indicate a wealthy urban household, consistent with the Acts 4 portrait of wealthy members supporting the community.

hist.context: The contrast between James and Peter in vv.1–17 is deliberately stark: James is executed immediately (v.2 — two words in Greek, 'he killed James with a sword'); Peter is imprisoned with maximum security and miraculously freed. Luke does not explain why James dies and Peter is freed — the silence is theological. Both outcomes are within God's sovereign will; neither is outside his governance. The prayer meeting's failure to believe it is Peter at the door (v.15) is Luke's gentle humour and his honest portrait of faith: the community prayed earnestly for Peter's release but was astonished when it happened.

sections.header: Verses 18—25 — Herod’s Death; The Word of God Spreads

heb.paragraph: Luke's favourite adverb of divine action — the same word used for healing miracles and Spirit-falling throughout Acts. Herod's refusal to give glory to God is met with immediate divine judgment. The contrast with the long-suffering patience shown toward Ananias and Sapphira (who had time to conspire) is deliberate.

heb.word: ὁ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ ηὔξανεν καὶ ἐπληθύνετο
heb.transliteration: ho logos tou theou ēuxanen kai eplēthyneto
heb.gloss: the word of God continued to spread and flourish
heb.paragraph: two imperfect verbs — ēuxanen (was growing, like a plant) and eplēthyneto (was multiplying). The growth is organic and ongoing: every attempt to stop the word ends with the word advancing further. This is Luke's theological refrain throughout Acts.

places.name: Caesarea Maritima — Herod's Court
places.text: Herod Agrippa's death in Caesarea is confirmed by Josephus (Antiquities 19.8.2), who describes the same event — a public address in a gleaming silver robe at a festival in honour of Caesar, followed by sudden illness and death five days later. Luke's account and Josephus's are independent and broadly consistent.

places.name: Tyre and Sidon
places.text: The great Phoenician port cities north of Caesarea — economically dependent on Galilee's food supply. Their delegation to Herod reflects the normal diplomatic relationships of the region. The occasion of their audience becomes the occasion of Herod's judgment.

tl.name: Herod Agrippa's Death
tl.text: Herod Agrippa I dies in Caesarea, struck down for accepting divine acclamation. His death removes the most powerful Jewish ruler since Herod the Great. The word of God immediately spreads and flourishes (v.24) — the contrast with Herod's silenced voice is deliberate.

refs.note: Ananias and Sapphira — the earlier founding-moment judgment. Both episodes follow the same pattern: human pride/deception → immediate divine judgment → church growth summary.

Josephus describes Herod Agrippa's death at a festival in Caesarea: wearing a silver robe that gleamed in the sun, acclaimed as divine, then suddenly struck with severe abdominal pain and dying five days later. The accounts are independent and broadly consistent.

"I am the LORD; that is my name! I will not yield my glory to another" — the theological principle behind Herod's judgment. God shares his glory with no one; to accept divine acclamation without deflecting it is to claim what belongs to God alone.

notes.note: MacArthur on Herod's judgment: the sin was not primarily political but theological — he received glory that belongs to God alone. MacArthur uses this as the permanent warning against any form of religious leadership that cultivates personal adulation rather than directing all honour to Christ. Herod accepted what Peter had refused (10:25–26: "Stand up, I am only a man").

MacArthur on the growth summary: Luke places this summary immediately after Herod's death as a deliberate theological statement. The persecutor is silenced; the word speaks on. This is the pattern of the entire book: every attempt to stop the Gospel ends in its acceleration. MacArthur calls this "the irony of persecution" — the church's most powerful growth has consistently occurred under the most determined opposition.

Calvin on divine judgment: God does not always judge immediately — he waited for Herod's ultimate act of arrogance before intervening. Calvin uses this to counsel against impatience when the wicked prosper. God's timing is not our timing; his silence is not absence; his delay is not indifference.

Calvin on 'the word of God continued to spread and flourish': this verse is, for Calvin, the summary of all church history. Kingdoms rise and fall; persecutors die; the word endures and advances. Calvin uses this as comfort for believers in Geneva facing Catholic opposition: 'Let kings and emperors boast, let them do what they will, the kingdom of Christ will stand unmoved.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that "eaten by worms" (skōlēkobrōtos) is a recognized ancient description of a gruesome death — used of other persecutors of God's people in Jewish literature (e.g., Antiochus Epiphanes in 2 Macc 9:9). The specific form of death is a literary-theological signal: the man who claimed divine glory dies in the most undignified way imaginable.

The NET notes this is the third of Luke's periodic growth summaries (after 6:7 and 9:31). Each summary marks the end of a narrative section and signals Spirit-blessed expansion. This one is the most dramatic because it immediately follows the death of the chapter's antagonist — the contrast is the theological point.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: "Because Herod did not give praise to God" — Robertson notes the immediate causal connection: the divine judgment is not for political persecution of Christians (though that was his crime) but for the specific sin of accepting divine acclamation without deflecting it to God. Pride in the moment of public glory is the proximate cause.

"The word of God continued to spread and flourish" — Robertson on the imperfect tenses: two verbs both in imperfect, both expressing ongoing, continuous action. Against the backdrop of Herod's sudden silencing, the word's continuing advance is the most pointed possible contrast.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Keener notes the close agreement between Luke and Josephus on Herod's death. Josephus specifies: a silver robe gleaming in sunlight, acclamation as divine, immediate severe abdominal pain, death five days later. Luke has: royal robes, "voice of a god," immediately struck by an angel, eaten by worms. The two accounts differ in details (timing, exact cause) but agree on the essential elements: Herod accepted divine honours and died very soon after.

John Mark's introduction here is significant: he is Barnabas's cousin (Col 4:10) and becomes the companion of Paul's first journey. His later departure (13:13) and eventual reconciliation (2 Tim 4:11) make him one of Acts' most humanly interesting supporting characters.

hist.context: Herod Agrippa's death is Acts' most dramatic divine judgment since Ananias and Sapphira (5:1–11). The structural parallel is intentional: both episodes involve the claim of divine status, both are met with immediate death, both are followed by church growth. The pattern is one of Luke's major theological themes: those who oppose the word die; the word lives. Josephus's independent account of Herod's death (Antiquities 19.8.2) provides the most direct external corroboration of any specific event narrated in Acts.

ppl.name: Peter↗ People
ppl.role: Lead apostle; spokesperson for the Jerusalem church
ppl.text: Peter (Simon) appears in John 1:40-42 when Andrew brings him to Jesus, who gives him the name Cephas (Peter). This is his first mention in John's Gospel.

ppl.name: James↗ People
ppl.role: Brother of Jesus; leader of the Jerusalem church
ppl.text: James the brother of Jesus (not the apostle James son of Zebedee) emerges in Acts as the dominant figure of the Jerusalem church. He is not mentioned in Acts 1–9 but appears decisively in Acts 12:17 (Peter reports to him after his prison release), acts as president at the Jerusalem Council (Acts 15:13–21), and receives Paul’s final Jerusalem report (Acts 21:18). Paul calls him a "pillar" of the church (Gal 2:9). He authored the letter of James.

ppl.name: Herod
ppl.text: MacArthur uses this to address the question of why God sometimes delivers and sometimes a MacArthur on earnest prayer: the contrast between the hopeless security of Peter's prison (four squads, two chains, two guards attached to him) and th…

ppl.name: Agrippa↗ People
ppl.role: Herod Agrippa II; king before whom Paul defends himself (Acts 26)
ppl.text: Herod Agrippa II is the last significant Herodian ruler, client-king of several territories and a man expert in Jewish customs and disputes (Acts 26:3). Paul’s defence before him in Acts 26 is the most polished forensic speech in Acts. Agrippa’s response — "In a short time you would persuade me to be a Christian?" (Acts 26:28) — is ambiguous: half-moved but unwilling to commit. He agrees with Festus that Paul could have been released had he not appealed to Caesar.

ppl.name: Ananias
ppl.text: MacArthur uses this to address the question of why God sometimes delivers and sometimes a MacArthur on earnest prayer: the contrast between the hopeless security of Peter's prison (four squads, two chains, two guards attached to him) and th…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThen the angel said to him, \ESVAnd the angel said to him, “Dress yourself and put on your sandals.” And he did so. And he said to him, “Wrap your cloak around you and follow me.”NIVSuddenly an angel of the Lord appeared and a light shone in the cell. He struck Peter on the side and woke him up. \ESVAnd behold, an angel of the Lord stood next to him, and a light shone in the cell. He struck Peter on the side and woke him, saying, “Get up quickly.” And the chains fell off his hands.

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (c.93 AD)
src.quote: Josephus provides extensive first-century background for the political, religious, and social conditions described in Acts — the Herodian family, Pharisees and Sadducees, Roman provincial governance.
src.note: Acts’ historical details consistently align with Josephus’s independent account of the same period, supporting the narrative’s historical credibility.

src.title: Dead Sea Scrolls (1QS, 1QM)
src.quote: The Qumran community texts illuminate the diversity of Second Temple Judaism from which Christianity emerged — their eschatological expectations, community rules, and temple criticism parallel aspects of the early church.
src.note: The Scrolls demonstrate that the early church’s practices (community meals, shared goods, eschatological expectation) were not unprecedented within Jewish renewal movements.

src.title: Greco-Roman Travel Literature and Epistolary Conventions
src.quote: Acts’ travel narrative (ch.13–28) conforms to the conventions of ancient Greek travel literature, and Paul’s speeches follow rhetorical models documented in Greco-Roman oratory.
src.note: The Hellenistic literary conventions confirm Acts’ composition for a sophisticated Greco-Roman audience and support its historical reliability as ancient historiography.

rec.who: John Chrysostom, Homilies on Acts (c.400 AD)
rec.text: "Chrysostom’s 55 homilies on Acts are the most thorough patristic commentary, emphasising the Spirit’s active role and the apostles’ moral example for the church."

tx.title: Western text (Codex Bezae D) vs Alexandrian text
tx.content: Alexandrian(P45, P74, א, B) is the primary scholarly text.Western(Codex Bezae D) is 10–15% longer, with additions that appear to expand and harmonise.
tx.note: Acts has the most significant NT textual variation. The Western text of Acts is not simply corrupt but may preserve early oral expansions. Most scholars follow the shorter Alexandrian text as more original.

tx.title: P45 and early papyrus witnesses
tx.content: The Chester Beatty Papyrus (P45, c.250 AD) is the earliest substantial Acts manuscript, generally supporting the Alexandrian text with some unique readings.
tx.note: The papyrus evidence has largely confirmed the Alexandrian tradition as the best text of Acts, though the Western text’s substantial additions remain a subject of scholarly investigation.

debate.title: Authorship and Historicity of Scripture Walkthrough

positions.name: Traditional Lukan authorship (Hengel, Keener, classical scholarship)
positions.proponents: Attributes Acts to Luke the physician (Col 4:14), a companion of Paul, on the basis of the "we" passages, historical accuracy, and early church tradition.
positions.argument: Luke’s medical vocabulary (noted by Hobart) and the detailed geographical and political accuracy of Acts support the traditional attribution.

positions.name: Anonymous/secondary authorship (Haenchen, Conzelmann)
positions.proponents: Questions Lukan authorship on the basis of discrepancies between Acts’ Paul and the Pauline letters, suggesting a later author using Lukan sources.
positions.argument: This view notes that Acts does not always match Paul’s letters precisely, and argues the "we" passages may be a literary device rather than eyewitness markers.

coaching.tip: 'So Peter was kept in prison, but the church was earnestly praying to God for him' (v. 5). An angel releases Peter; Rhoda forgets to open the door. The church prays for release, then disbelieves the answer.

'Herod did not give praise to God, and an angel of the Lord struck him down' (v. 23). The king who killed James and imprisoned Peter dies eaten by worms. 'But the word of God continued to spread' (v. 24). Contrast complete.

prayer_prompt: As the church prays earnestly for Peter's release and then cannot believe it when he shows up at the door, consider asking God whether you pray bold prayers and then are surprised when He answers them.
